title: Glossary
description: A collection of technical terms and their definitions

terms.definition: The low-level contract (calling conventions, data layout) between compiled components or between a guest and the hypervisor.
An ordered list of rules granting or denying access to a resource.
Open standard for hardware discovery, configuration, and power management exposed by firmware to the OS.
Low-level firmware that initialises server hardware before the OS boots; its power and Turbo settings can affect VM performance.
Several NICs combined into one logical network link for redundancy or higher throughput.
The standard EFF command-line client that requests and renews free Let's Encrypt (ACME) TLS certificates.

The standard tool that performs early initialization of a cloud VM on first boot — users, SSH keys, hostname, growing the root disk. XO can inject a cloud-init config when creating a VM from a template.
An open-source cloud-orchestration platform that can manage XCP-ng hosts as a hypervisor backend.
The background process that merges a chain of incremental disk snapshots back into the base disk after backups or snapshots. It is random-I/O heavy and can dominate disk load on slow storage.
Technique that shares data until a write occurs, then copies only the changed block; used by snapshots and thin storage.
The x86 instruction (and the feature data it returns) by which software and the hypervisor discover CPU capabilities.
A request bundle, containing a public key and identity, submitted to a CA to obtain a certificate.
The standard identifier scheme for publicly disclosed security vulnerabilities.
The Linux distribution commonly used as the base OS when installing Xen Orchestra from sources (rather than using the XOA appliance).
A backup that stores only the blocks changed since the last full backup, reducing time and storage.
Protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses and network settings to hosts.
Hardware feature letting devices read/write memory without the CPU; the reason an IOMMU is needed for safe passthrough.
The distributed system that resolves names to IP addresses.
The first VM created by Xen, to run the management toolstack
A regular VM running under the control of Dom0
Libraries for fast user-space packet processing that bypass the kernel network stack.
A distributed replicated storage system for the Linux platform
Firmware interface between platform firmware and the OS; see UEFI, its modern form.
The point after which a product or version no longer receives updates or support.
Community repository of add-on packages for RHEL and derivatives.
Intel's hardware-assisted nested paging for VMs (AMD's equivalent is NPT).
VMware's bare-metal hypervisor; the main platform XCP-ng migrants leave.
An alternative local SR type in XCP-ng that uses thin provisioning, meaning disk space is only consumed as data is written. Named after the common Linux file system.
Software distributed under licences granting use, study, modification, and redistribution.
XCP-ng background process that reclaims storage used by orphaned snapshot chains after backup or replication jobs.
Red Hat's shared-disk cluster filesystem allowing concurrent access from multiple nodes.
Open implementation of OpenPGP for signing and encryption; used for package and release signing.
The copyleft licence requiring derivative works to remain under the same terms.
Specialised processor; in virtualisation it can be passed through or shared to VMs, with scheduler modes trading fairness against throughput.
A tunnelling protocol that wraps arbitrary network-layer packets inside IP.
The common Linux bootloader that loads the kernel (and the Xen hypervisor).
A card connecting a host to storage/network fabric (e.g. Fibre Channel, SAS).
The list of hardware validated to work with a platform.
HVM refers to a set of extensions that make it much simpler to virtualize the processor
The layer that runs virtual machines; in XCP-ng this is Xen plus the Dom0 control domain.
Standards body for electrical and electronic technologies (e.g. ISO/IEC 27001).
Professional body and standards organisation (e.g. IEEE 802 networking).
terms.abbreviation: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers

terms.definition: Virtualization of device I/O, most often via SR-IOV virtual functions.
Open standard (PICMG) for out-of-band server management. Defines the protocol that BMCs speak. ipmitool is the common CLI client.
Long-standing text chat protocol used by many open-source communities.
International standards body; also the common name for an optical-disc image file.
A JSON-based wire protocol for remote procedure calls (RPC), defining only a few data types and commands.
A key used to encrypt other keys; in UEFI Secure Boot, the key authorising updates to the signature database.
The Linux in-kernel hypervisor; the basis of many competing virtualization stacks.
Protocol that bundles multiple links into one logical bonded interface.
A network confined to a single site or building.
Management layer from LINBIT that sits above DRBD. Handles provisioning, snapshots, and placement of replicated storage volumes across a cluster. The control plane for XOSTOR.
A release line that receives fixes and support for an extended period.
A number used to identify a logical unit
A Linux disk management layer. In XCP-ng, LVM SRs are thick-provisioned — each VDI pre-allocates its full declared size.
The hardware address identifying a network interface on a LAN.
The legacy disk partitioning and boot scheme preceding GPT/UEFI.
The largest packet size a network link will carry; mismatches cause fragmentation or drops.
File-level storage served over the network (e.g. NFS/SMB).
Rewriting of IP addresses/ports as traffic crosses a boundary.
Protocol exposing a block device over the network; used by Xen Orchestra to stream disk data during backups.
Standard protocol (originally Sun/RFC 1813) for sharing directories over a network so remote machines can mount them as local filesystems. One of the supported SR types in XCP-ng.
A physical network port on a server. A bond combines several NICs into one logical link.
Microsoft Windows' native filesystem. Reading or writing it on a Linux host (such as XCP-ng dom0) needs the ntfs-3g driver, which is not installable from dom0's default repos.
Keeps server clocks synchronised over a network; clock drift between hosts can cause subtle storage, backup, and authentication failures.
On multi-socket servers, memory attached to one CPU socket is faster for that socket; keeping a VM's CPUs and memory in the same NUMA node improves performance.
A fast storage interface protocol used by modern SSDs, operating over PCIe. Can be passed through directly to a VM for near-bare-metal storage performance.
A company whose hardware is sold or rebranded by another vendor; a hardware-partner channel.
The condition where a system exhausts available RAM; the Linux kernel's OOM killer then terminates processes to recover memory.
Software released under a licence permitting use, modification, and redistribution.
A single-file (tar) packaging of an OVF virtual machine for distribution.
Open-source UEFI firmware (built from EDK II) used to boot virtual machines; packaged here for XCP-ng.
The software-defined virtual switch used by XCP-ng to manage VM network traffic.
The management protocol/schema for configuring Open vSwitch.
Process of converting a physical machine into a VM, typically by imaging its disks and rebuilding the boot chain inside the hypervisor.
The XAPI object connecting an XCP-ng host to a storage repository.
The bus standard (PCIe) for attaching devices; the unit of device passthrough. (In payments, PCI also = PCI DSS.)
The index of a physical memory page; central to Xen's memory mapping.
XAPI term for a physical network port on a host. Every real NIC that XCP-ng can see shows up as a PIF object in the XAPI database. Compare with VIF (virtual equivalent).
A small build demonstrating feasibility before fuller investment.
A group of XCP-ng hosts that share configuration, storage, and a single XAPI database. Operations like VM migration and shared SRs are scoped to a pool.
A network setting that lets a virtual interface receive all traffic on its segment, not just frames addressed to it.

A Xen guest mode where a modified guest kernel makes hypercalls to the hypervisor instead of using hardware virtualization extensions. Xen's original lightweight mode; fully removed in XCP-ng 8.3 in favour of HVM and PVH.
An HVM guest that loads paravirtual (PV) drivers for fast disk/network I/O.
Standard for booting a machine from the network.
QEMU's modern PCIe-based emulated chipset for VMs (vs the legacy i440FX).
A disk image format used by XCP-ng that supports thin provisioning and snapshots more efficiently than the older VHD format.
Open-source machine emulator/virtualizer; provides device emulation for HVM guests under Xen.
Combining disks for redundancy and/or performance.
Authorization model granting permissions to roles, which are assigned to users.
An architectural style for HTTP APIs using resources and standard verbs.
Red Hat's commercial Linux distribution; a common guest OS.
CPU design philosophy with simple, regular instructions; the basis of RISC-V.
Invoking a procedure in another address space or host as if local.
The package format and tool used by RHEL/CentOS and XCP-ng's dom0.
XCP-ng/XAPI procedure that patches a pool host-by-host, evacuating VMs to avoid downtime.
A widely-used public-key cryptosystem.
Object storage protocol popularized by AWS; XO supports any S3-compatible provider for backup remotes.
Enterprise drive interface, higher reliability and throughput than SATA but slower than NVMe; common in rack servers.
Decoupling network control from forwarding so networks are programmable.
AMD feature encrypting a VM's memory to isolate it from the hypervisor; of interest to Xen confidential computing.
File transfer over an SSH-secured channel.
A family of cryptographic hash functions (SHA-1/2/3).
XAPI's storage-layer interface; the daemon that manages SR lifecycle, scans, and VDI operations.
The first-generation XCP-ng storage data path (tapdisk-based); the older, slower route newer storage work aims to move past.
Microsoft file-sharing protocol (also called CIFS). One of the supported share types for ISO SRs in XCP-ng.
Log file for SMAPI activity at /var/log/SMlog; on busy pools this is typically the dominant /var/log consumer.
AMD SEV-SNP feature adding memory-integrity protection for confidential VMs; relevant to Xen guest support.
User-space libraries for high-performance, polled-mode storage I/O.
The standard language for relational databases.
XAPI term for a storage target — what other hypervisors call a "datastore." Can be local disk, NFS, iSCSI, FC, or XOSTOR. VDIs live inside an SR.
An RPM package containing source code and the spec to build binary RPMs.
Flash-based storage device with no moving parts.
The legacy name (now TLS) for the protocol securing network connections.
A virtual Ethernet device the hypervisor uses to connect a VM's NIC to a host bridge.
The reliable, connection-oriented transport protocol of the IP suite.
A minimal UDP file-transfer protocol, commonly used in PXE boot.
Allocating storage space on demand as data is written, rather than reserving the full disk size up front. Used by NFS and local-ext SRs (not LVM-based block SRs).
Encryption layer for network connections (HTTPS, LDAPS, etc.); modern successor to SSL.
A readable configuration file format.
A hardware (or virtual) root of trust storing keys and measurements; required by Windows 11 guests.
A high-resolution CPU cycle counter; its handling is critical during VM live migration.
The connectionless, best-effort transport protocol of the IP suite.
Modern platform firmware interface replacing legacy BIOS; supports Secure Boot.
A string identifying a resource (URLs are a kind of URI).
The primary time standard; clock skew between hosts causes subtle failures.
A 128-bit identifier; XAPI keys VMs, hosts, and SRs by UUID.
Converting/moving a VM from one hypervisor to another (e.g. VMware to XCP-ng).

XAPI object connecting a VDI to a VM, presenting the virtual disk to the guest as a physical block device. Holds attachment details such as device position, bootable flag and read/write mode. The network equivalent is a VIF.
A CPU presented to a guest VM; too few vCPUs starves parallel workloads such as backup workers, and oversubscription across hosts can hurt performance.
XAPI term for a single virtual disk (what VMware calls a VMDK, and what libvirt calls a volume). VDIs live inside an SR and are attached to VMs as virtual block devices.

An LVM construct that pools one or more physical volumes into a single allocation space for logical volumes. XCP-ng LVM-type SRs create a VG on the storage device and carve out VDIs as logical volumes.
Legacy display standard; the basic emulated graphics adapter for VMs.
Nvidia's mechanism for slicing one physical datacentre GPU (T4, A10, L4, etc.) across multiple VMs; requires a supported card and a GRID license.
Microsoft disk-image file format historically used by XCP-ng EXT-based SRs; superseded by QCOW2 in XCP-ng 8.3+ for the 2TiB-limit removal.
XAPI term for a virtual NIC attached to a VM. Each VIF is bridged to a network (which maps to a physical PIF or bond). Compare with PIF (physical equivalent).
A way to segment network traffic at the switch/bridge level so different groups of devices appear to be on separate networks.
A guest operating system running on the hypervisor.
VMware's virtual-disk format.
Remote-framebuffer protocol used for VM consoles.
An encrypted tunnel extending a private network across untrusted links.
VMware's hyper-converged software-defined storage.
A software-emulated TPM chip required by Windows 11 for secure boot and disk encryption. XCP-ng 8.3 introduced vTPM support.
Overlay encapsulating Layer-2 over UDP/IP to scale tenant networks beyond VLAN limits.
A network spanning sites or regions.
The management toolstack (daemon + REST/XML-RPC API) that controls XCP-ng hosts. Every operation — VM creation, storage, networking — goes through XAPI. `xe` CLI and Xen Orchestra both talk to it.
The original open-source build of Citrix XenServer (2010-2013). Discontinued once XenServer itself was open-sourced, then revived in 2018 as XCP-ng ("next generation").
The open-source Xen-based hypervisor that runs the virtual machines.
The legacy Windows desktop client for XenServer/XCP-ng management, maintained by Citrix/Cloud Software Group.
Citrix's commercial Xen-based hypervisor; XCP-ng is the open-source platform that shares much of its lineage.
A high-performance journaling file-system; an option for dom0/host storage.
a remote procedure call (RPC) protocol which uses XML to encode its calls and HTTP as a transport mechanism.
terms.abbreviation: Extensible Markup Language Remote Procedure Call

terms.definition: The open-source web management interface for XCP-ng (and XenServer). Replaces XenCenter. Provides the Console tab (noVNC), backup jobs, resource scheduling, and the XAPI REST proxy.

The pre-packaged virtual appliance version of Xen Orchestra distributed by Vates. Deploys as a ready-to-run VM rather than requiring a manual source build. Includes paid features (advanced backup, DR) not in the open-source edition.
Hyper-convergence solution based on LINSTOR
Official Xen Project security advisories documenting vulnerabilities and fixes.
XCP-ng/XenServer's VM export/import archive format.
A copy-on-write filesystem with integrity checking, snapshots, and RAID-like features.
